question: What does the blackboard show about the structure of a proof by contradiction, and how does the lecturer connect this to the example of proving the irrationality of square root of two?
answer: The blackboard shows that proof by contradiction requires assuming P is false (i.e., not-P is true) and then deriving a contradiction. The lecturer connects this to the square root of two example by assuming square root of two is rational, expressing it as a/b in lowest terms, deriving that both a and b must be even, and concluding that a/b was not in lowest terms — a contradiction, thereby proving square root of two is irrational.

ground_truth_spans.description: Blackboard content showing proof by contradiction structure: assume P is false, use hypothesis to derive contradiction
Lecturer explains how proof by contradiction applies to proving square root of two is irrational, assuming it is rational leads to contradiction

question: What does the blackboard display about the theorem on the sum of natural numbers, and how does the inductive step shown on the board complete the proof?
answer: The blackboard displays the theorem that for all n greater than or equal to 0, the sum 1 plus 2 plus 3 up to n equals n times (n+1) divided by 2. The inductive step shown on the board assumes P(n) is true — that is, 1 plus 2 up to n equals n(n+1)/2 — and then rewrites 1 plus 2 up to n plus (n+1) as n(n+1)/2 plus (n+1), which simplifies to (n+1)(n+2)/2, confirming P(n+1) is true and completing the proof.

ground_truth_spans.description: Blackboard shows theorem statement: for all n>=0, sum from 1 to n equals n(n+1)/2
Blackboard shows inductive step rewriting sum as n(n+1)/2 plus (n+1) to get (n+1)(n+2)/2

answer: The slide displayed on the projection screens shows the theorem statement as 'Theorem (NOT!): 90 greater than 92' with the subtitle 'Proof: by PowerPoint,' and describes the approach of taking two triangles with total area 90 and dividing them into four triangles with total area greater than 92 to imply via the conservation of area axiom that an area of 90 is larger than an area of 92.

ground_truth_spans.description: Slide shown on projection screens: Theorem (NOT!): 90 > 92, Proof by PowerPoint, description of triangle area argument

question: According to the lecturer, what were the two axioms of the Pythagoreans that together implied square root of two was rational, and what happened when a proof of its irrationality was discovered?
answer: The Pythagoreans held two axioms: first, that there were no irrational numbers — they simply did not exist; and second, that every length of a line was finite and therefore rational. Together these axioms, combined with the Pythagorean theorem showing that a right triangle with legs of length one has a hypotenuse of square root two, implied square root two must be rational. When a proof that square root two is irrational was discovered, it meant their axioms were inconsistent, every theorem they had proved became suspect, and according to legend they killed the person who revealed this result to prevent the destabilizing knowledge from spreading.

ground_truth_spans.description: Lecturer explains Pythagorean axioms: no irrational numbers exist, and every constructible line length is rational
Lecturer explains discovery of irrationality proof caused inconsistency, suspect theorems, and according to legend the discoverer was killed

question: According to the lecturer, what two gods did the Pythagorean religious society worship, and what did each god represent?
answer: The Pythagorean religious society worshipped two gods: Aperon, who was the bad god and the god of infinity — because infinity was considered all that was bad — and Peros, who was the good god and the god of the finite world, representing everything that was good to the ancient Greeks.

ground_truth_spans.description: Lecturer describes the two Pythagorean gods: Aperon the bad god of infinity and Peros the good god of the finite world

question: What story does the lecturer tell about the Stata Center building to motivate the courtyard tiling problem, and what specific architectural feature created unexpected costs?
answer: The lecturer explains that the Stata Center, the building the lecture is taking place in, was originally supposed to cost under 200 million dollars fully furnished. MIT hired Frank Gehry as architect, and the cost quickly exceeded 300 million dollars — a figure the lecturer confirms is literally true — because all the slanted walls and unusual architectural features turned out to be very expensive to build. Fundraising became a huge priority once they were more than 200 million dollars over budget and had not yet purchased furniture, leading to the fictional proposal of building a large courtyard with a statue of a wealthy donor in the center.

ground_truth_spans.description: Lecturer recounts Stata Center cost overrun story: originally under $200M, Frank Gehry hired, costs exceeded $300M due to slanted walls, fundraising crisis led to courtyard statue idea

question: The frame caption for the proof-by-contradiction section shows 'To prove P is true, we assume P is False (i.e., ¬P is True) and then use that hypothesis to derive a contradiction.' How does the lecture then apply exactly this structure when proving that the square root of 2 is irrational?
answer: The frame caption explicitly states the contradiction method: assume ¬P (i.e., that √2 is rational), then derive a contradiction. The lecture applies this by assuming √2 = a/b in lowest terms, squaring to get 2b²=a², showing a must be even, then showing b must also be even, contradicting the assumption that a/b was in lowest terms. This contradiction proves √2 is irrational.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption showing the formal proof by contradiction statement on blackboard
Lecture applies contradiction to prove irrationality of sqrt(2) by showing both a and b are even

question: The blackboard frame caption for the divisibility theorem shows 'Thm: ∀n ∈ N, 3 | (n^3 - n)' with a base case of n=0. What does the inductive step involve, and how does the lecture use the inductive hypothesis to complete the proof?
answer: The theorem states that 3 divides n³-n for all natural numbers n. In the inductive step, the lecture expands (n+1)³-(n+1) to get n³+3n²+3n+1-n-1, then rewrites it as (n³-n)+3n²+3n. Since 3 divides n³-n by the inductive hypothesis P(n), and 3 divides 3n² and 3 divides 3n trivially, the entire expression is divisible by 3, completing the proof.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption showing the theorem statement 3 divides n^3-n on the blackboard
Lecture explains using inductive hypothesis to show three divides the expanded expression

question: The lecture slide visible in the frame shows the theorem about tiling a 2^n × 2^n courtyard with a center square missing. How does the lecture modify the inductive hypothesis to make the proof work, and why does a stronger hypothesis make the proof easier?
answer: The original hypothesis that Bill must be in the center was too restrictive for the inductive step. The lecture changes the hypothesis to state that any square (not just the center) can be missing. With this stronger P(n), when proving P(n+1) for a 2^(n+1)×2^(n+1) courtyard, you divide into four 2^n×2^n blocks, place Bill in one block, put an L-shaped tile at the center junction covering one square from each of the other three blocks, then apply P(n) to all four blocks independently. The stronger hypothesis gives more to assume, making the recursive application straightforward.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption showing theorem about tiling 2^n x 2^n region with central square missing
Lecture explains strengthening the inductive hypothesis to any square missing makes proof easier

question: What two key axioms did the Pythagoreans hold, and how did the proof that √2 is irrational create a contradiction with both of them simultaneously?
answer: The Pythagoreans held two key axioms: first, that there were no irrational numbers—they simply did not exist; and second, that every length of a line was finite and therefore rational. The Pythagorean theorem itself showed that a right triangle with two sides of length 1 has a hypotenuse of length √2. Since √2 is a constructible length, the second axiom implied it must be rational. But the proof by contradiction showed √2 is irrational, contradicting the first axiom. Thus both axioms were violated simultaneously, making their entire system of theorems suspect.

ground_truth_spans.description: Lecture states the Pythagorean axiom that irrational numbers do not exist and their belief in only finite rational numbers
Lecture explains axiom that every line length is rational and how Pythagorean theorem forced sqrt(2) to be rational

question: According to the lecture, what was the historical consequence for the person who disclosed the proof that √2 is irrational to the outside world, and what does the lecturer say about the credibility of this story?
answer: According to the lecture, there was a 'deep throat'—someone who let out the word and the proof that √2 is irrational, because this result would be very destabilizing for Pythagorean society. According to legend, they killed him for disclosing it. The lecturer notes this is the legend and explicitly says it is hard to imagine getting killed over the irrationality of two, signaling uncertainty about the story's literal truth while presenting it as a historical legend.

ground_truth_spans.description: Lecture recounts the legend that the person who disclosed the irrationality proof was killed and notes it is hard to imagine
